title: Common Atrial Flutter and Macroreentrant and Multifocal Atrial Tachyca
authors: William H. Sauer, Jorge E. Romero, Paul C. Zei

items.subtopic: Common Atrial Flutter and Macroreentrant and Multifocal Atrial Tachycardias
items.title: Mechanism and Diagnosis of Common Atrial Flutter
items.content: Common atrial flutter involves a reentry circuit around the tricuspid valve annulus, often counterclockwise. It is characterized by specific ECG findings: negative sawtooth waves in leads II, III, and aVF, and positive P waves in lead V. The typical atrial rate ranges from 240–300 beats/min, with a common 2:1 AV block leading to ventricular rates of 140–150 beats/min.
keyPoints: Common atrial flutter is cavotricuspid isthmus-dependent
Counterclockwise rotation produces negative sawtooth waves in leads II, III, and aVF
Atrial rate typically 240–300 beats/min; ventricular rates often 140–150 beats/min due to 2:1 AV block
Diagnosis aided by maneuvers increasing AV nodal block
items.reference: Common Atrial Flutter and Macroreentrant and Multifocal Atrial Tachyca · Common Atrial Flutter and Macroreentrant and Multifocal Atrial Tachycardias

items.subtopic: Common Atrial Flutter and Macroreentrant and Multifocal Atrial Tachycardias
items.title: Differentiation and Management of Atypical Atrial Flutters
items.content: Atypical atrial flutters, unlike common flutter, do not depend on the cavotricuspid isthmus and are associated with atrial scars. They can occur post-cardiac surgery or ablation for atrial fibrillation. Distinguishing these from focal AT often requires an electrophysiology study due to varied P-wave morphologies.
keyPoints: Atypical flutters are not cavotricuspid isthmus-dependent
Associated with atrial scars, common post-cardiac surgery or ablation
Differ in P-wave morphology from typical flutter
Diagnosis often requires electrophysiology study
items.reference: Common Atrial Flutter and Macroreentrant and Multifocal Atrial Tachyca · Common Atrial Flutter and Macroreentrant and Multifocal Atrial Tachycardias

items.title: Why Catheter Ablation is First-Line Therapy for Atrial Flutter
items.content: Catheter ablation is favored as the first-line therapy for atrial flutter due to its high success rate, low complication risk, and reduced recurrence rates. While antiarrhythmic drug therapy has a recurrence rate of over 70%, catheter ablation boasts a success rate greater than 95%. However, up to half of patients may develop other atrial arrhythmias post-ablation.
keyPoints: Catheter ablation is first-line due to >95% success rate and low complications
>70% recurrence with antiarrhythmic drugs
Up to 50% of patients developing other atrial arrhythmias post-ablation
items.reference: Common Atrial Flutter and Macroreentrant and Multifocal Atrial Tachyca · ATRIAL FLUTTER

items.title: Role of Anticoagulation in Atrial Flutter Management
items.content: Anticoagulation is essential for thromboembolic prevention in patients with atrial flutter, guided by the CHA2DS2-VASc score and bleeding risk. It should be initiated before converting to sinus rhythm unless immediate cardioversion is needed due to hemodynamic instability or severe symptoms.
keyPoints: Anticoagulation based on CHA2DS2-VASc score
Crucial for thromboembolic prevention before rhythm conversion
Immediate cardioversion may be warranted in unstable patients
items.reference: Common Atrial Flutter and Macroreentrant and Multifocal Atrial Tachyca · ATRIAL FLUTTER

items.title: Characteristics and Management of Multifocal Atrial Tachycardia (MAT)
items.content: Multifocal atrial tachycardia is identified by the presence of at least three distinct P-wave morphologies on an ECG, with a heart rate ranging from 100 to 150 beats per minute. It differs from atrial fibrillation as it shows clear isoelectric intervals between P waves and generally has a slower atrial rate. MAT often results from triggered automaticity due to multiple atrial foci and is frequently seen in patients with chronic pulmonary disease or acute illnesses.
keyPoints: At least three distinct P-wave morphologies
Heart rates typically 100-150 beats/min
Clear isoelectric intervals between P waves
Slower atrial rate compared to atrial fibrillation
Common in chronic pulmonary disease and acute illnesses
Triggered automaticity from multiple atrial foci
items.reference: Common Atrial Flutter and Macroreentrant and Multifocal Atrial Tachyca · MULTIFOCAL ATRIAL TACHYCARDIA

items.title: Therapeutic Approach for Multifocal Atrial Tachycardia (MAT)
items.content: The management of MAT focuses on treating the underlying condition and correcting metabolic abnormalities. Electrical cardioversion is ineffective in MAT. Calcium channel blockers like verapamil or diltiazem can be used to control heart rate, while beta-blockers are often poorly tolerated by patients with severe pulmonary disease. Amiodarone may be effective but is generally avoided for long-term use due to its potential toxicities, including pulmonary fibrosis. The risk of thromboembolism in MAT is not well-defined and differs from that associated with atrial fibrillation or flutter.
keyPoints: Treat underlying disease and metabolic abnormalities
Electrical cardioversion ineffective
Use calcium channel blockers (verapamil, diltiazem) for rate control
Beta-blockers poorly tolerated in severe pulmonary disease
Amiodarone may be used but avoid long-term due to toxicity risks
Thromboembolism risk unclear and differs from atrial fibrillation/flutter
items.reference: Common Atrial Flutter and Macroreentrant and Multifocal Atrial Tachyca · MULTIFOCAL ATRIAL TACHYCARDIA

items.subtopic: Common Atrial Flutter and Macroreentrant and Multifocal Atrial Tachycardias
items.question: What is the typical atrial rate for common or typical right atrial flutter?
items.explanation: The typical atrial rate for common or typical right atrial flutter is 240–300 beats/min.
items.reference: Common Atrial Flutter and Macroreentrant and Multifocal Atrial Tachyca · Common Atrial Flutter and Macroreentrant and Multifocal Atrial Tachycardias

items.subtopic: Common Atrial Flutter and Macroreentrant and Multifocal Atrial Tachycardias
items.question: Which anatomical structure bounds the wavefront anteriorly in common atrial flutter?
items.explanation: The tricuspid valve annulus bounds the wavefront anteriorly in common atrial flutter.
items.reference: Common Atrial Flutter and Macroreentrant and Multifocal Atrial Tachyca · Common Atrial Flutter and Macroreentrant and Multifocal Atrial Tachycardias

items.subtopic: Common Atrial Flutter and Macroreentrant and Multifocal Atrial Tachycardias
items.question: What is a characteristic feature of reverse typical atrial flutter?
options: Positive sawtooth flutter waves in leads II, III, and aVF
Negative P waves in lead V
items.explanation: Reverse typical atrial flutter is characterized by clockwise rotation.
items.reference: Common Atrial Flutter and Macroreentrant and Multifocal Atrial Tachyca · Common Atrial Flutter and Macroreentrant and Multifocal Atrial Tachycardias

items.question: What is considered first-line therapy for achieving long-term rhythm control in patients with atrial flutter?
options: Rate control with AV nodal-blocking agents
items.explanation: Catheter ablation is recognized as first-line therapy due to its high success rate and low recurrence rates.
items.reference: Common Atrial Flutter and Macroreentrant and Multifocal Atrial Tachyca · ATRIAL FLUTTER

items.question: Which scoring system is used to determine the need for anticoagulation in patients with atrial flutter?
items.explanation: The $ CHA_2DS_2 $-VASc scoring system is used to assess thromboembolic risk.
items.reference: Common Atrial Flutter and Macroreentrant and Multifocal Atrial Tachyca · ATRIAL FLUTTER

items.question: What percentage of patients experience recurrences after antiarrhythmic drug therapy for atrial flutter?
items.explanation: More than 70% of patients experience recurrences after antiarrhythmic drug therapy.
items.reference: Common Atrial Flutter and Macroreentrant and Multifocal Atrial Tachyca · ATRIAL FLUTTER

items.question: Which of the following features distinguishes multifocal atrial tachycardia (MAT) from atrial fibrillation?
options: Presence of at least three distinct P-wave morphologies with clear isoelectric intervals between them.
Isoelectric intervals are absent between P waves.
Atrial rate tends to be faster than in atrial fibrillation.
items.explanation: MAT is characterized by distinct P-wave morphologies and clear isoelectric intervals, unlike atrial fibrillation.
items.reference: Common Atrial Flutter and Macroreentrant and Multifocal Atrial Tachyca · MULTIFOCAL ATRIAL TACHYCARDIA

items.question: Which of the following medications may be used to manage MAT but should generally be avoided for long-term therapy due to potential toxicities?
items.explanation: Amiodarone may respond in MAT but is avoided long-term due to potential toxicities like pulmonary fibrosis.
items.reference: Common Atrial Flutter and Macroreentrant and Multifocal Atrial Tachyca · MULTIFOCAL ATRIAL TACHYCARDIA

items.question: Which patient population often does not tolerate beta-blocker therapy well when managing MAT?
items.explanation: Patients with severe pulmonary disease often do not tolerate beta-blocker therapy well in MAT.
items.reference: Common Atrial Flutter and Macroreentrant and Multifocal Atrial Tachyca · MULTIFOCAL ATRIAL TACHYCARDIA

items.subtopic: Common Atrial Flutter and Macroreentrant and Multifocal Atrial Tachycardias
items.statement: Maneuvers that increase AV nodal block will typically obscure flutter waves, making diagnosis difficult.
items.explanation: Increasing AV nodal block usually exposes flutter waves, aiding in diagnosis.
items.reference: Common Atrial Flutter and Macroreentrant and Multifocal Atrial Tachyca · Common Atrial Flutter and Macroreentrant and Multifocal Atrial Tachycardias

items.subtopic: Common Atrial Flutter and Macroreentrant and Multifocal Atrial Tachycardias
items.statement: Antiarrhythmic drugs such as flecainide can promote reentry by slowing atrial conduction velocity.
items.explanation: Flecainide slows atrial conduction, which can promote reentry and lead to atrial flutter.
items.reference: Common Atrial Flutter and Macroreentrant and Multifocal Atrial Tachyca · Common Atrial Flutter and Macroreentrant and Multifocal Atrial Tachycardias

items.statement: Prophylactic cavotricuspid isthmus ablation is beneficial in preventing new-onset atrial fibrillation.
items.explanation: While it may reduce the risk of recurrent atrial flutter, its benefit in preventing new-onset atrial fibrillation is not established.
items.reference: Common Atrial Flutter and Macroreentrant and Multifocal Atrial Tachyca · ATRIAL FLUTTER

items.statement: Rate control in atrial flutter can be effectively achieved with AV nodal-blocking agents.
items.explanation: AV nodal-blocking agents are used for rate control, though it may be more challenging compared to atrial fibrillation.
items.reference: Common Atrial Flutter and Macroreentrant and Multifocal Atrial Tachyca · ATRIAL FLUTTER

items.statement: Electrical cardioversion is an effective treatment for multifocal atrial tachycardia.
items.explanation: Electrical cardioversion is ineffective in treating MAT.
items.reference: Common Atrial Flutter and Macroreentrant and Multifocal Atrial Tachyca · MULTIFOCAL ATRIAL TACHYCARDIA

items.statement: The risk of thromboembolism in MAT is considered to be the same as that in atrial fibrillation.
items.explanation: The associated risk of thromboembolism in MAT is not considered to be the same as in atrial fibrillation.
items.reference: Common Atrial Flutter and Macroreentrant and Multifocal Atrial Tachyca · MULTIFOCAL ATRIAL TACHYCARDIA

items.subtopic: Common Atrial Flutter and Macroreentrant and Multifocal Atrial Tachycardias
items.assertion: Atypical atrial flutters are almost universally associated with areas of atrial scar.
items.reason: They can occur in either atrium and often follow cardiac surgery or extensive ablation procedures.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the association.
items.reference: Common Atrial Flutter and Macroreentrant and Multifocal Atrial Tachyca · Common Atrial Flutter and Macroreentrant and Multifocal Atrial Tachycardias

items.assertion: Catheter ablation is increasingly favored as the first-line therapy for atrial flutter.
items.reason: It has a high success rate, low risk of complications, and low recurrence rates.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why catheter ablation is favored.
items.reference: Common Atrial Flutter and Macroreentrant and Multifocal Atrial Tachyca · ATRIAL FLUTTER

items.assertion: Multifocal atrial tachycardia (MAT) is often observed in patients with chronic pulmonary disease.
items.reason: The underlying mechanism of MAT involves triggered automaticity from multiple atrial foci, which can be exacerbated by chronic pulmonary conditions.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, but the reason is not the direct explanation for the observation in patients with chronic pulmonary disease.
items.reference: Common Atrial Flutter and Macroreentrant and Multifocal Atrial Tachyca · MULTIFOCAL ATRIAL TACHYCARDIA
